hook: A quiet baroque square whose church wall is still pitted with shrapnel from a 1938 air raid that killed dozens of children sheltering inside.
facts: On 30 January 1938, during the Spanish Civil War, bombs fell on the square where the Sant Felip Neri church was being used as a shelter; a first bomb killed about 30 people, most of them children, and a second bomb that hit rescuers brought the toll to around 42.
The pockmarked scarring visible on the church facade is shrapnel damage from the bombing, not bullet marks from executions as is sometimes claimed.
The raid was part of the aerial bombardment of Barcelona; the city's democratic-memory programme attributes the attack to Franco's Italian allies (the Aviazione Legionaria).
The secluded baroque square sits behind the cathedral in the Gothic Quarter and is named for the 18th-century church of Sant Felip Neri.
w: A hush falls in this baroque courtyard behind the cathedral -- until you see the church wall, still pitted with shrapnel. On 30 January 1938 a Francoist bomb struck the square while children sheltered inside; a second caught the rescuers, and around forty died. Not a firing squad's bullets, as the myth runs. The scars of an air raid.

hook: Four nine-metre Roman columns stand hidden in a medieval courtyard at the highest point of the ancient city, all that survives of Barcino's forum temple.
facts: The four surviving columns (around 9-10 m tall) are the remains of a temple from the 1st century BC that stood in the forum of the Roman colony of Barcino, traditionally associated with the imperial cult of Augustus.
They are tucked inside a 15th-century building at Carrer del Paradís 10, which houses the Centre Excursionista de Catalunya, reached through an unremarkable medieval doorway and courtyard.
A millstone set in the courtyard floor opposite no. 10 marks Mont Tàber, the small hill (about 16-17 m high) that was the highest point of the Roman city.
The columns form part of the Museu d'Història de Barcelona (MUHBA) and can be visited free of charge.
w: Through a plain medieval doorway on Carrer del Paradis, four nine-metre Roman columns rise in a courtyard -- all that stands of the forum temple of Barcino, raised to the imperial cult in the first century BC. A millstone in the floor marks Mont Taber, the little hill that crowned Roman Barcino. Two thousand years, hidden in plain sight.

hook: A cramped basement on Carrer Marlet is believed to be one of the oldest surviving synagogue sites in Europe, rediscovered only in the late 20th century.
facts: The Sinagoga Major sits at Carrer de Marlet 5 in El Call, Barcelona's medieval Jewish quarter, and is described as the oldest in Spain and one of the oldest synagogue sites in Europe, with remains reaching back to Roman times.
Its location was identified by medievalist Jaume Riera i Sans, who from 1987 reconstructed the route of a medieval tax collector that ended at the Sinagoga Major.
After centuries of secular use following the destruction of the Call in the 1391 pogrom, the building reopened as a synagogue and museum in 2002.
El Call was Barcelona's walled Jewish quarter until the Jews were expelled and the community dissolved at the end of the 14th century.
w: A cramped basement on Carrer Marlet, believed one of the oldest surviving synagogue sites in Europe, with stonework reaching back to Roman times. Secular for six centuries after the 1391 pogrom destroyed the Call, its location was traced only in 1987, by following the route of a medieval tax collector. It reopened as a synagogue in 2002.

hook: A neo-Gothic stone bridge arching over Carrer del Bisbe hides a skull pierced by a dagger on its underside, fuel for a city curse legend.
facts: The covered bridge was built in 1928 by architect Joan Rubió i Bellver, a former collaborator of Antoni Gaudí, ahead of the 1929 International Exhibition.
It links the Palau de la Generalitat (the Catalan government palace) to the Casa dels Canonges across Carrer del Bisbe, between Plaça de Sant Jaume and Plaça Nova.
On its underside is carved a skull transfixed by a dagger, which has become the focus of various urban legends.
Despite its medieval Gothic appearance, the bridge is a 20th-century addition to the quarter.
legend: One legend holds that if the dagger is ever removed from the skull the city of Barcelona will collapse; another says a wish made while walking backwards beneath the bridge, eyes fixed on the skull, will come true.
It is also said the frustrated architect, angry that only this one of his proposed Gothic-quarter interventions was built, carved the skull as a curse on those who pass beneath.
w: The neo-Gothic bridge arching over Carrer del Bisbe looks medieval; Rubio i Bellver built it in 1928. Look up at its underside: a stone skull pierced by a dagger. The story goes that pulling the dagger out would topple Barcelona -- or that the snubbed architect carved it as a curse on all who pass below.

hook: An eternal flame burns over a mass grave beside Santa Maria del Mar, where the defenders who fell in the 1714 siege of Barcelona were buried.
facts: The square stands over a cemetery (the 'graveyard of the mulberry trees') where citizens killed defending Barcelona during the 1714 siege in the War of the Spanish Succession were buried in a mass grave.
A steel torch holding an eternal flame, designed by Alfons Viaplana, was installed in 2001 in memory of the fallen.
It sits immediately beside the Gothic basilica of Santa Maria del Mar in La Ribera.
The site is a national symbol of Catalonia and a focus of commemorations on the Diada, Catalonia's national day (11 September), which marks the city's fall in 1714.
w: An eternal flame leans over a paved square beside Santa Maria del Mar -- the 'graveyard of the mulberry trees', where the defenders who fell in the 1714 siege were buried in a mass grave. Viaplana's steel torch was lit in 2001. Every 11 September, Catalonia's national day, the flowers pile up here. A nation remembers its fall.

n: El Born Centre de Cultura i Memòria
hook: Beneath a grand 19th-century iron market hall lie the excavated streets of the La Ribera district, razed after the 1714 siege to build a fortress.
facts: The cast-iron market hall was designed by Antoni Rovira i Trias and built in the 1870s; it was one of Europe's largest covered markets and served as Barcelona's wholesale produce market until 1971.
During its conversion to a library in the early 2000s, builders uncovered the well-preserved foundations of the La Ribera neighbourhood as it was around 1700.
That quarter was demolished after the 1714 siege to clear ground for the Bourbon Ciutadella fortress, displacing its residents.
The site reopened in 2013 as El Born CCM, a culture and memory centre built around the exposed archaeological ruins; documentary records even allowed the families who lived in the houses to be named.
w: Under a grand iron market hall of the 1870s lie the excavated streets of La Ribera, as they stood around 1700. The Bourbons razed the quarter after the 1714 siege to clear ground for a fortress; builders rediscovered it while converting the market to a library. Records even name the families who lived there. A neighbourhood exhumed.

a: Sant Pere, Santa Caterina i la Ribera
hook: A market crowned by a billowing wave of coloured ceramic tiles, built over a demolished medieval convent and Roman-era remains.
facts: Originally opened in the 1840s on the site of the demolished Dominican convent of Santa Caterina, it was one of Barcelona's first covered markets.
A 2005 remodelling by architects Enric Miralles and Benedetta Tagliabue (EMBT) added the undulating roof clad in roughly 325,000 hexagonal ceramic tiles in dozens of colours.
Excavations during the redevelopment exposed remains including the old convent and earlier archaeological layers, now displayed within the market complex.
It stands in the Sant Pere, Santa Caterina i la Ribera quarter of Ciutat Vella, on Avinguda Francesc Cambó.
w: A billowing wave of ceramic -- some 325,000 hexagonal tiles in many colours -- rolls above this market, Miralles and Tagliabue's 2005 roof over a much older site. Beneath it lie the foundations of a Dominican convent razed in the 1830s and Roman-era layers, now on show among the stalls. Barcelona's oldest covered market, re-dressed.

hook: A seven-metre, two-tonne bronze cat with comically swollen proportions lounges down the middle of Rambla del Raval.
facts: The sculpture is a monumental bronze cat by Colombian artist Fernando Botero, rendered in his trademark rounded, oversized forms; it measures about seven metres long and weighs around two tonnes.
It arrived in Barcelona in 1987 and was moved between several locations before being given a permanent home on the newly created Rambla del Raval in 2003.
It has become an informal mascot of the multicultural Raval district.
legend: A local superstition holds that touching the cat brings good luck.
w: Seven metres of swollen bronze, two tonnes of comically fat cat, lounging down the middle of the Rambla del Raval. Botero's beast wandered the city from 1987, shuffled between sites, before settling here in 2003 as the unofficial mascot of the multicultural Raval. Rub it, the locals say, for luck.

n: Antic Hospital de la Santa Creu
hook: A serene Gothic courtyard in the Raval, once Catalonia's great medieval hospital, now shelters the National Library and is where Gaudí died.
facts: Founded in 1401 to unify Barcelona's existing hospitals, it became the largest hospital in Catalonia and is one of the finest examples of Catalan civil-Gothic architecture, built between the 15th and 17th centuries.
Since 1939 its halls have housed the Biblioteca de Catalunya (the national library of Catalonia), alongside the Institut d'Estudis Catalans and art schools.
Antoni Gaudí was brought here after being struck by a tram in 1926 and died in the hospital days later.
The complex is entered from Carrer de l'Hospital 56 and is a declared national monument, with a tranquil orange-tree courtyard open to the public.
w: A tranquil orange-tree courtyard off the Raval opens into Catalonia's great medieval hospital, founded in 1401 and built in soaring civil-Gothic stone over three centuries. Its halls have held the national library of Catalonia since 1939. Here, in 1926, they brought Antoni Gaudi after a tram struck him, and here he died.

hook: Puig i Cadafalch fused three sisters' houses into one spiky neo-Gothic 'castle' marooned on a Diagonal island, crowned with six conical-spired towers.
facts: Built 1903-1905 by Josep Puig i Cadafalch for the three daughters of industrialist Bartomeu Terradas i Mont, at Avinguda Diagonal 416-420.
Its six pointed towers topped with conical spikes gave it the popular nickname 'Casa de les Punxes' (House of Spikes).
It is the only Modernista building in Barcelona that is freestanding on all four sides, occupying its own triangular block.
A large ceramic panel on one facade depicts Sant Jordi (St George), a hallmark of the Renaixenca medievalism Puig i Cadafalch championed.
Declared a national historic-artistic monument in 1976.
legend: Local lore reads the building as the dragon of the St George legend turned to stone, its tile-scaled roof and spired towers the slain beast — a story the building's own visitor experience leans into.
w: Puig i Cadafalch welded three sisters' houses into one spiky neo-Gothic castle, marooned on its own triangular island off the Diagonal bristling with six spired towers -- the only Modernista building in Barcelona free on all four sides. A ceramic Sant Jordi guards one facade. Local lore reads the whole pile as the dragon turned to stone.

n: Palau del Baró de Quadras (Casa Asia / Institut Ramon Llull)
hook: A building with two faces: a carved neo-plateresque North-European palace toward the Diagonal, a plainer tenement toward Carrer Rosselló behind.
facts: Puig i Cadafalch remodelled an existing apartment block for Baron Manuel de Quadras between 1902 and 1904; entrance at Avinguda Diagonal 373.
The ornate Diagonal balcony carries busts of medieval and Renaissance figures, heraldic shields and floral motifs, with sculpture by Eusebi Arnau and Alfons Juyol.
It housed Casa Asia from 2003, and since 2013 has been home to the Institut Ramon Llull, which promotes Catalan language and culture abroad.
Declared a national historic-artistic monument in 1976.
w: A building with two faces. Toward the Diagonal, Puig i Cadafalch carved a North-European palace dense with busts, shields and floral stone; slip round to Carrer Rossello and it shrinks to a plain tenement. Arnau cut the sculpture in 1904. It has promoted Catalan culture abroad ever since, latterly as the Institut Ramon Llull.

hook: A Modernista house with a split personality — a sober stone Diagonal front, and a riotous polychrome ceramic-and-wood gallery facade hiding around the back on Carrer Corsega.
facts: Designed by Salvador Valeri i Pupurull and built 1909-1911 for Joan Comalat, on the triangular block where Avinguda Diagonal (no. 442) meets Carrer de Corsega (no. 316).
The Diagonal facade is symmetrical undulating stone; the rear Corsega facade is asymmetric, polychrome and lined with curved wooden galleries and louvered blinds.
It is among the most Gaudi-influenced of Barcelona's home-grown Modernista buildings, its curves an homage to organic Gaudinian form.
The two facades are so different they are often mistaken for two separate buildings.
w: One house, two personalities. The Diagonal front runs in sober undulating stone; walk around to Carrer Corsega and a riot of polychrome tile and curved wooden galleries breaks out, so unlike the front it reads as a different building. Valeri i Pupurull built it 1909-11, his curves a frank homage to Gaudi. The back nobody expects.

hook: A white-stucco Puig i Cadafalch palace whose carved entrance hides a self-portrait of the architect — riding a bicycle.
facts: Built 1898-1901 by Josep Puig i Cadafalch for businessman Roma Macaya i Gibert at Passeig de Sant Joan 108.
On the carriage-entrance capital, sculptor Eusebi Arnau carved a man on a bicycle that is understood to depict Puig i Cadafalch himself, who cycled to the building site.
The white facade combines stone sculpture by Eusebi Arnau, sgraffito by Joan Paradis and wrought iron by Manuel Ballarin.
La Caixa bought the building in 1947; today it runs as a free cultural centre hosting talks and exhibitions on social transformation.
Declared a Cultural Asset of National Interest in 1973.
w: A white-stucco palace on Passeig de Sant Joan, all sgraffito and carved stone, with a private joke over the door: on the entrance capital, Eusebi Arnau sculpted a man on a bicycle -- Puig i Cadafalch himself, who pedalled to the site. La Caixa runs it now as a free cultural centre. Architecture with the architect winking back.

hook: An 1888 cast-iron market hall whose flower stalls never close — the only corner of the Eixample where you can buy roses at 4am.
facts: Built 1887-1888 to a design by Antoni Rovira i Trias, its metal framework supplied by the celebrated Barcelona engineering firm La Maquinista Terrestre i Maritima; main entrance at Carrer d'Arago 311-317.
The basilica-plan hall has a wide central nave flanked by two lower aisles, carried on 84 cast-iron columns — an early example of modular metal market architecture in the city.
The flower market along the Carrer de Valencia side is open 24 hours a day, 365 days a year, unusual for the neighbourhood.
A 1996-1998 renovation by Albert de Pineda added underground parking and glass-and-shutter enclosures while keeping the iron structure.
w: An 1888 cast-iron market hall in the Eixample, its 84 columns cast by the great Maquinista works -- and the one corner of the district where you can buy roses at four in the morning. The flower stalls along Carrer de Valencia trade around the clock, every day of the year. Iron, glass, and petals that never sleep.

n: Jardins de la Torre de les Aigues ('la Platja de l'Eixample')
hook: Tucked inside an Eixample city block, an 1860s brick water tower presides over a hidden sand-and-pool 'beach' that locals queue for in summer.
facts: Reached through a passage at Carrer de Roger de Lluria 56, the courtyard sits in the block bounded by Roger de Lluria, Consell de Cent and Diputacio.
The tower was raised to supply water under pressure to the first houses of Cerda's new Eixample; a well was dug in the courtyard in 1867 and the tower heightened around 1870.
Barcelona opened the recovered interior as a public garden in 1987 — one of the first of the Eixample's reclaimed block interiors.
Each summer the council lays sand, a wooden walkway and a shallow pool (about 60 cm deep), nicknamed 'the beach of the Eixample', with capacity for a few hundred people.
w: Through a passage off Carrer de Roger de Lluria hides one of the Eixample's secret block interiors: an 1860s brick water tower over a courtyard where, each summer, the city lays down sand, a boardwalk and a shallow pool. The locals call it the beach of the Eixample, and queue for it. A seaside, walled inside a city block.

hook: A cobbled, gated lane of garden-fronted villas cutting through an Eixample block — a fragment of the leafy 'English' city Cerda dreamed of but Barcelona never built.
facts: Laid out in 1864 by architect Jeroni Granell i Barrera, the passage crosses the block framed by Roger de Lluria, Pau Claris, Diputacio and Consell de Cent.
It is named after Francesc Permanyer i Tuyets, who was mayor of Barcelona in 1856.
Two rows of unified single-family houses, each with a small front garden, line a central cobbled path — among the best surviving English-style terraces in the city.
Past residents included pianist Carles Vidiella and the poet-illustrator Apel·les Mestres; many houses are now offices.
Cerda had envisaged many such garden-lined mid-block passages, but the city largely abandoned the idea, leaving Permanyer a rare survivor.
w: A gated cobbled lane slips through an Eixample block, lined with two rows of garden-fronted villas -- a fragment of the leafy English-style city Cerda dreamed of and Barcelona mostly never built. Granell laid it out in 1864; a pianist and a poet once lived here. One of the last survivors of an idea the grid swallowed whole.

n: Torre del Rellotge de Gracia (Placa de la Vila de Gracia)
hook: Gracia's 33-metre clock tower, whose bell rang for six days under cannon fire during an 1870 anti-conscription revolt and still bears the scar.
facts: The octagonal tower was built 1862-1864 by Antoni Rovira i Trias, with a clock mechanism by Swiss watchmaker Albert Billeter, in what was then the independent town of Gracia.
During the 1870 'Quintes' (conscription) revolt, residents who refused to send their sons to the army rang the bell continuously as a call to resist while the army shelled the square.
A commemorative plaque records the 1864 construction, the 1870 uprising damage and the 1882 restoration.
The bell is nicknamed 'La Marieta' and remains a symbol of Gracia's tradition of resistance.
Gracia was an independent municipality until it was annexed to Barcelona in 1897.
legend: Tradition holds that a local woman warned the neighbourhood the army was coming by ringing the bell via a rope that reached up to her rooftop, and that a cannonball cracked the bell, permanently altering its sound even after repair.
w: Gracia's 33-metre clock tower presides over the square that bears the town's name. In the 1870 revolt against conscription, neighbours rang its bell, La Marieta, for days while the army shelled the square. The story goes a cannonball cracked it, altering its voice ever after. A bell that called a town to resist.

hook: Gracia's wrought-iron 'Freedom Market', roofed by Gaudi's right-hand man and still the buzzing larder of the neighbourhood.
facts: The market opened in 1888 on the former Placa de la Constitucio, where farmers had long sold produce, and was given its iron-and-glass roof in 1893.

The covering and ironwork were the work of Francesc Berenguer i Mestres, Gaudi's long-time assistant, who also designed the coat of arms; the original building was by Gracia municipal architect Miquel Pasqual i Tintorer.
It has a three-aisle plan with a high central nave, glazed-ceramic pitched roofs on a metal structure and bare brick walls pierced for light and air.
It was extensively restored and modernised around 2009 while keeping its Modernista ironwork.
w: Gracia's 'Freedom Market', its wrought-iron roof thrown up in 1893 by Francesc Berenguer -- Gaudi's right-hand man, who also drew its coat of arms. Three brick-and-iron aisles under a high glazed nave, still the buzzing larder of the neighbourhood after a careful 2009 restoration. Modernista ironwork, doing its day job.

n: El Petó de la Mort (Cementiri de Poblenou)
hook: In Barcelona's old seaside cemetery, a winged marble skeleton leans down to kiss a swooning young man on the brow.
facts: The marble group, 'El petó de la mort' (The Kiss of Death), was carved in 1930 and depicts a winged skeleton kissing the forehead of a young man.
It marks the grave of Josep Llaudet Soler, a textile manufacturer, in the Cementiri de Poblenou.
The work is signed by the Jaume Barba workshop but is attributed by family sources to the designer Joan Fontbernat (Barba's son-in-law).
The plinth carries verses by the Catalan poet-priest Jacint Verdaguer on youth, faltering blood and the kiss of death.
The Cementiri de Poblenou opened in 1775 (rebuilt after Napoleonic destruction in 1819) on what was then marshland east of the city, the first municipal cemetery outside the walls.
w: In Barcelona's old seaside cemetery, a winged marble skeleton leans down to kiss a swooning young man on the brow -- 'the Kiss of Death', carved in 1930 over a textile heir's grave. Verdaguer's verses on faltering blood run round the plinth. Among the city's most photographed tombs, and its most unsettling.

n: Fossar de la Pedrera (Cementiri de Montjuïc)
hook: A disused Montjuïc quarry became a mass grave for thousands shot by Franco's regime, and now a silent memorial garden holds President Companys.
facts: The Fossar de la Pedrera, an old quarry within the Cementiri de Montjuïc, was used as a mass grave for an estimated 4,000 victims of Francoist repression after the Civil War.
Lluís Companys, the 123rd President of the Generalitat de Catalunya, was executed at Montjuïc Castle on 15 October 1940 and his remains were interred here.
A memorial of name-inscribed columns, a garden of tombstones and Companys's mausoleum was designed by architects Beth Galí, Marius Quintana and Pere Casajoana.
The memorial was inaugurated on 27 October 1985 in the presence of President Jordi Pujol and Barcelona mayor Pasqual Maragall.
Entrance inscriptions to the victims were written by the writer Maria Aurèlia Capmany.
w: A worked-out Montjuic quarry became a pit for the dead -- some 4,000 victims of Franco's firing squads thrown in after the war. President Lluis Companys, shot at the castle above in 1940, lies here. In 1985 Beth Gali turned the hollow into a garden of name-carved columns and silence. Catalonia's wound, kept open on purpose.

hook: A Greek amphitheatre carved into a Montjuïc quarry on a French architect's whim, now the open-air heart of the city's summer festival.
facts: The Teatre Grec was built in 1929 for the Barcelona International Exposition, using the slope of the disused 'Machinet' (la Foixarda) quarry on Montjuïc.
It was designed by architects Ramon Reventós and Nicolau Maria Rubió i Tudurí, modelled on the ancient theatre of Epidaurus, with the carved quarry wall serving as backdrop.
The open-air theatre seats roughly 1,900 spectators.
The idea is credited to French landscape architect Jean-Claude Nicolas Forestier, who on a 1921 walk past the quarry remarked it would make a good Greek theatre.
Since 1976 it has been the principal venue of the Grec, Barcelona's summer festival of theatre, dance, music and circus.
w: A French landscape architect walked past a disused Montjuic quarry in 1921 and said it would make a fine Greek theatre. By 1929 it had become one -- Reventos and Rubio modelling it on Epidaurus, the cut rock face left as the backdrop. Some 1,900 seats under the open sky. Each summer it turns into the beating heart of the Grec festival.

hook: The red windmill sails of Paral·lel's old cabaret still turn at night, a survivor of Barcelona's risqué music-hall heyday.
facts: El Molino is a historic cabaret and music hall on Avinguda del Paral·lel (Carrer de Vila i Vilà 99) in Poble Sec, marked by an illuminated red windmill on its facade.
The venue opened in 1908 as the 'Petit Moulin Rouge', echoing the Paris original.
During the Franco era it dropped 'rouge'/'red' from its name and became simply 'El Molino' (the windmill).
Paral·lel was historically nicknamed Barcelona's 'Broadway' for its concentration of theatres, cabarets and nightlife.
After a long closure the building was restored and reopened in 2010 with its windmill sails relit.
w: The red windmill sails on Avinguda del Paral·lel still turn at night, lit again since 2010 -- the sign of a cabaret that opened in 1908 as the Petit Moulin Rouge. Under Franco it quietly dropped the 'rouge'. Here ran the raffish Broadway of Barcelona, a strip of theatres and music halls. The sails kept the memory turning.

hook: One of the world's oldest still-working observatories, where you can dine under the stars and peer through a 1904 telescope that found its own comet.
facts: The Fabra Observatory was inaugurated in 1904 on a Tibidabo ridge, funded by a bequest from industrialist Camil Fabra, first Marquis of Alella.
It was designed by architect Josep Domènech i Estapà and inaugurated in the presence of King Alfonso XIII; it belongs to the Royal Academy of Sciences and Arts of Barcelona.
It is among the oldest observatories in the world still in operation, devoted mainly to the study of asteroids and comets.
Astronomer Josep Comas i Solà discovered the periodic comet 32P/Comas Solà and numerous asteroids from here, using the 1904 Mailhat double refractor (two 38 cm aperture tubes).
Since the early 2000s the observatory has hosted summer 'Sopars amb estrelles' (dinners with the stars) combining a meal with telescope observing.
w: One of the oldest working observatories on earth, opened on a Tibidabo ridge in 1904 with a king in attendance. Through its 1904 double refractor, Comas i Sola found a comet that now bears his name. Domenech i Estapa gave it a domed temple of a building. On summer nights you can dine beneath the dome, then put your eye to the old glass.

hook: A neo-Gothic basilica crowned by a bronze Christ with arms flung wide, perched on the peak whose name means 'I will give you'.
facts: The Temple Expiatori del Sagrat Cor stands on the summit of Tibidabo, Barcelona's highest point (about 512 m).
It was built between 1902 and 1961, designed by architect Enric Sagnier and completed by his son Josep Maria Sagnier i Vidal, in neo-Byzantine and neo-Gothic styles.
The church is two superimposed churches, a lower crypt church (1903-1911) and an upper church (1915-1951).
It is crowned by a large statue of the Sacred Heart of Jesus; the original 1935 bronze was destroyed in 1936 at the start of the Civil War and replaced by Josep Miret in 1950.
The mountain's name derives from the Latin 'tibi dabo' ('I will give you'), the Gospel words of the Devil's temptation of Christ, echoed in the church's stained glass.
Pope John XXIII granted it the rank of minor basilica on 29 October 1961.
legend: By tradition the name Tibidabo recalls the Gospel temptation in which Satan, showing Christ the kingdoms of the world from a high place, says 'haec omnia tibi dabo' - 'all this I will give you' - if he will kneel and worship him.
w: On Barcelona's highest peak stands a neo-Gothic basilica crowned by a bronze Christ, arms wide over the city. Sagnier and his son raised it across sixty years, 1902 to 1961. The mountain's name comes from the Devil's words in the Gospel temptation -- 'tibi dabo', I will give you -- spoken from just such a height. Faith with a funicular.

hook: A little red biplane that has spun on its own propeller above Barcelona since 1928 - billed as the world's first flight simulator.
facts: L'Avió is a red plane attraction at the Tibidabo amusement park that has operated since 1928, driven around its pivot by its own working propeller.
It is a replica of the aircraft that made the first Barcelona-to-Madrid flight in 1927.
The amusement park itself, founded by entrepreneur Salvador Andreu, opened in 1905 and is among the oldest still operating in the world.
The Avió became the park's emblem and is described as the first-ever flight simulator; it still preserves original parts such as a 1933 radio-telephone set.
In January 2017 it was dismantled for the first time after 88 years of continuous operation, for restoration that preserved its original structure.
w: A little red plane has spun on its own propeller above Tibidabo since 1928, spun round like a fairground ride -- a replica of the aircraft that first flew Barcelona to Madrid in 1927. The park beneath it, opened in 1905, ranks among the world's oldest. Billed as the first flight simulator. Aloft 88 years before its first repair.
